Short Description: GFP is the jellyfish protein that made living cells glow green and transformed biology as a lab tool. It works beautifully as a reporter across many organisms, but visible fluorescence does not penetrate human tissue well and is not a therapy.
Mechanism: 238-aa 11-β-strand barrel; autocatalytic S65-Y66-G67 chromophore; requires only O2 (no cofactor/substrate).
Achievements (effect sizes): Universal reporter across E. coli, yeast, plants, C. elegans, Drosophila (Chalfie 1994 Science); zebrafish; Tg mice (Okabe 1997 "green mice" CAG-EGFP ubiquitous); Tg pigs (Hwang/Lai 2006); Tg marmoset with germline transmission (Sasaki 2009 Nature); Tg cats (Wongsrikeao 2011 Nat Methods, co-expressed with TRIMCyp, FIV-resistant); routine in every human cell line. EGFP ε~56,000 M−1cm−1, QY 0.6, single-cell detection. c3GFP photostable mutant (2020) permits STED super-resolution.
Highest Evidence Tier: T6 (human cells routinely); T5 across multiple mammalian species; T7 ex-vivo only (not approved in vivo in humans)
Translational Gaps: Clinical in-vivo reporter role ceded to HSV1-TK ([18F]FHBG PET), NIS, PSMA, eDHFR/DYR-TMP.
Key References (DOIs): Chalfie Science 1994 https://doi.org/10.1126/science.8303295 | Okabe FEBS Lett 1997 https://doi.org/10.1016/S0014-5793(97)00313-X | Sasaki Nature 2009 https://doi.org/10.1038/nature08090
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: Reporter, not enhancement; green emission doesn't penetrate tissue; O2-dependent maturation; photobleaching; mildly immunogenic in humans — cleared from adoptive-cell therapies. Human clinical imaging uses PET reporters, not GFP.

Narrative: A firefly lights up by burning a tiny molecule called luciferin. The enzyme luciferase is astonishingly efficient — about 41% of the chemical energy becomes visible photons, a quantum yield that human engineering cannot match. Put luciferase into a mouse tumor, inject luciferin, and you can count cancer cells through living tissue. Enhanced versions can track as few as three adoptively transferred T-cells. For decades it was the workhorse of biomedical imaging. The frontier now is autonomous bioluminescence — getting mammals to make their own luciferin so they glow without injections. Autonomous plants already exist (a 2020 fungal-pathway tobacco produces visible light on its own, ~10⁸ photons per second per plant), but mammals lack a key precursor. The dream of glowing tattoos or bioluminescent skin runs aground on the same problem that made GFP a lab tool and not a therapy: tissue absorbs visible light. A glowing human would glow, pharmacologically speaking, only skin-deep.
Short Description: Firefly luciferase makes light by burning luciferin, letting researchers watch cells inside living animals after giving the substrate. It is powerful for imaging, but a self-glowing mammal still faces the hard limits of fuel supply and light blocked by tissue.
Mechanism: ATP+Mg+O2-dependent oxidation of D-luciferin → ~560 nm photon (QY ≈ 0.41).
Achievements (effect sizes): de Wet 1987: firefly luc in mammalian cells. Ow 1986 Science: first glowing tobacco (exogenous luciferin spray). Tg luc+ mice are a staple — IVIS imaging after 150 mg/kg IP D-luciferin; detect tumors from ~1,000 cells. Rabinovich 2008 PNAS: enhanced firefly luciferase (effLuc) expressed in mouse T-cells enabled bioluminescent tracking of as few as 3 individual cells in adoptive immunotherapy models. Close 2010/2018 PNAS: autonomous bacterial luxCDABEfrp in HEK293 cells — 12× background, visible at 25,000 cells with 10-min integration. Mitiouchkina 2020 Nat Biotechnol: fungal nnluz + hispS/h3h/npgA gene cluster from Neonothopanus nambi in tobacco → autonomous visible bioluminescence ~10^8–10^9 photons/s per plant (endogenous caffeic acid → luciferin).
Highest Evidence Tier: T6 (human cells substrate-dep); T5 (mice, luciferin-dep); T3 (autonomous in plants); NOT fully autonomous in peer-reviewed mammals yet
Confidence: Very High (luciferin-dep); High (autonomous plants); Low-Medium (autonomous mammals)
Translational Gaps: In-human clinical imaging ceded to PET reporters; autonomous mammal germline line not peer-reviewed.
Key References (DOIs): de Wet Mol Cell Biol 1987 https://doi.org/10.1128/mcb.7.2.725-737.1987 | Ow Science 1986 https://doi.org/10.1126/science.234.4778.856 | Rabinovich PNAS 2008 https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0804105105 | Mitiouchkina Nat Biotechnol 2020 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-020-0500-9
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: Classical system requires substrate injection; mammalian caffeic acid scarce → low autonomous flux; tissue absorption; ATP-coupled signal. Bacterial lux vs fungal luz debate — codon-optimized lux may outperform fungal in mammals lacking caffeic acid.

Narrative: The electric eel generates 860 volts by stacking thousands of modified muscle cells — electrocytes — that discharge in unison. The molecular machinery is a teleost-specific duplicate of the muscle sodium channel, scn4aa, which lost its original job in muscle and was repurposed to fire on demand inside the electric organ. It's a beautiful case of evolutionary tinkering. In 2018, Harold Zakon's group took five amino-acid changes from an Apteronotus knifefish and engineered them into a human cardiac sodium channel expressed in frog eggs. The resulting human channel generated a large, physiologically relevant persistent sodium current without needing the usual accessory proteins. But the 860-volt discharge isn't a protein — it's an anatomical design, the electrical equivalent of batteries wired in series across five thousand cells. No mammalian cell or mouse makes its own electricity. The hydrogel 'artificial electric organs' that periodically make the press are pure materials science: zero genes, 110 volts, impressive engineering, no biology.
Short Description: Electric fish repurposed sodium-channel genes like scn4aa as part of organs that fire thousands of cells in series. The channel biology is fascinating, but the impressive voltage comes from anatomy, not from one protein acting alone.
Mechanism: Teleost-WGD Nav paralog; lost muscle expression, compartmentalized to electrocytes; positively selected substitutions in inactivation gate / S4–S5 linker; electric organ voltage arises from STACKED series of electrocytes firing via Nav channels.
Achievements (effect sizes): Zakon 2006 PNAS: convergent muscle-expression loss + positive selection. Gallant 2014 Science: eel genome + muscle-vs-EO transcriptomics. Traeger 2015 BMC Genomics: Nav isoform quantification. Thompson 2018 PLoS Biol: Apteronotid 5-residue D4 S4–S5 linker substitutions engineered into HUMAN hNav1.5 backbone, expressed in Xenopus oocytes → large persistent Na+ current (INaP) gain-of-function, independent of auxiliary beta-subunits. Native eel protein was NOT expressed in mammalian cells. Whole-animal E. voltai >860 V is ANATOMICAL (stacked electrocytes). Schroeder 2017 Nature "artificial electric organ" is hydrogel (110 V, ~27 mW/m2), zero genetic content.
Translational Gaps: No mammalian cell/mouse bioelectrogenesis; electrocyte developmental program un-transferred.
Key References (DOIs): Zakon PNAS 2006 https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0600160103 | Thompson PLoS Biol 2018 https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.2004892 | Schroeder Nature 2017 https://doi.org/10.1038/nature24670
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: Voltage = MULTICELLULAR ARCHITECTURE, not single gene. Channel identity is actually scn4ab1 in Apteronotids (not scn4aa) — complicating "universal electric-organ gene" narrative. Apteronotids retain muscle scn4aa. Artificial organs (Schroeder 2017) sidestep biology entirely.

Narrative: A gecko can hang from a glass ceiling by a single toe because its foot-pads are covered in millions of microscopic hairs — setae — each tipped with even finer spatulas. The contact area gets so large that van der Waals forces alone are enough. No glue, no suction. The 2015 Gekko japonicus genome revealed a family of about 72 beta-keratin genes that make these setae, with bursts of duplication around 100 and 85 million years ago — matching the evolutionary origins of gecko adhesion. A single seta generates 20 to 40 micronewtons of force, and a whole foot can hold 40 times the animal's body weight. Synthetic gecko-inspired tapes, from polymer microfibers to carbon-nanotube arrays, already work reasonably well. But expressing gecko beta-keratins in mammalian cells to grow actual setae is a non-starter: the adhesion depends on a hierarchical anatomy — cells, pillars, hairs, spatulas — built by a developmental program, not on the protein sequence itself.
Short Description: Gecko beta-keratin proteins help build the microscopic hairs that let gecko feet stick by van der Waals forces. The stickiness comes from a precise hierarchy of skin structures, so expressing the protein alone would not grow a gecko toe pad.
Mechanism: Large multigene family (~71–72 CBPs); polymerize into ~3–4 nm β-sheet filaments in oberhautchen/beta-layer cells → setae → spatular tips (~200–500 nm); adhesion is PURELY van der Waals.
Achievements (effect sizes): Liu 2015 Nat Commun: G. japonicus genome (2.55 Gb); 72 CBP genes mapped to setae; phylogenetic expansions ~105–96 Mya and ~87–80 Mya. Alibardi IHC: Ge-cprp-9 in setae/spatular tips. Autumn 2000 Nature: single seta 20–40 μN; whole foot holds ~40× body weight. Autumn 2002 PNAS: van der Waals confirmed on polar + nonpolar surfaces; synthetic PDMS/polyester spatulae reproduce adhesion. ZERO heterologous CBP transfer producing functional setae. Gecko tape (Geim 2003 Nat Mater) + CNT gecko tapes (Qu 2008 Science) are SYNTHETIC — no protein, no gene.
Confidence: High (source biology); High that gene transfer won't produce setae (morphogenetic barrier)
Translational Gaps: Gene transfer essentially a category error — adhesion requires hierarchical nanoarchitecture from developmental program, not compositional.
Key References (DOIs): Autumn Nature 2000 https://doi.org/10.1038/35015073 | Autumn PNAS 2002 https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.192252799 | Liu Nat Commun 2015 https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms10033 | Geim Nat Mater 2003 https://doi.org/10.1038/nmat917
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: Setae also contain lipids + α-keratins. CBP count disputed (26 vs 72 annotations) — underannotated repetitive family. Adhesion requires hierarchical nanoarchitecture from developmental program — not compositional.

Narrative: Your muscles are locked in a tug-of-war between growth signals and a powerful brake called myostatin. Follistatin is nature's way of releasing that brake — it binds myostatin and neutralises it, unleashing muscle growth that is otherwise tightly suppressed. In 2009, Haidet and Bhatt at Nationwide Children's Hospital showed that a single intramuscular injection of AAV1-follistatin-344 increased muscle mass and grip strength in both normal and dystrophic mice, with quadriceps weight rising over 100% in some cohorts. In 2015, Mendell's group ran the first human trial: six boys with Becker muscular dystrophy received bilateral AAV1-FS344 injections into the quadriceps. The six-minute walk test improved by a mean of 58 metres at 24 months, with no serious adverse events and no detectable anti-myostatin antibodies. A 2017 follow-up in inclusion body myositis patients showed similar safety and modest functional benefit. The catch: follistatin also binds activins and other TGF-beta family members, meaning its effects are not muscle-specific — it influences reproductive hormones, inflammation, and fibrosis in ways that are hard to predict long-term.
Short Description: Follistatin-344 neutralises the muscle-growth brake myostatin. AAV gene therapy trials in muscular dystrophy patients showed safety and modest functional improvement, but follistatin's broad activity across TGF-beta family members makes long-term effects unpredictable.
Mechanism: Follistatin: 344-aa secreted glycoprotein; binds and neutralises myostatin (GDF-8), activin A/B, and GDF-11 with high affinity; blocks SMAD2/3 signalling through ActRIIB; relieves transcriptional repression of myogenic genes → satellite cell activation, myofiber hypertrophy.
Achievements (effect sizes): Haidet 2009 (AAV1-FS344 mouse): quadriceps mass +100% vs controls; grip strength +60% in mdx mice. Mendell 2015 (human phase I/IIa, n=6 Becker MD): 6-min walk +58 m at 24 months (3/6 patients improved >50 m); no SAEs; anti-AAV1 Abs present but no anti-myostatin Abs. Mendell 2017 (IBM, n=6): safe; 2/6 improved on 6MWT. Kota 2009 (AAV8-FS315 mouse): muscle mass +73%, specific force +12%.
Translational Gaps: Systemic delivery for whole-body effect; long-term reproductive/endocrine effects of activin blockade; phase III efficacy trials pending; dose–response in healthy muscle vs dystrophic; potential bone density effects.
Key References (DOIs): Haidet Proc Natl Acad Sci 2009 https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0709144104 | Mendell Mol Ther 2015 https://doi.org/10.1038/mt.2015.36 | Mendell Neurology 2017 https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000004485
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: Follistatin is NOT myostatin-specific — it also neutralises activins (reproductive hormones) and GDF-11 (disputed aging factor). Long-term endocrine consequences unknown. FST-344 includes a heparin-binding domain that limits systemic distribution vs FST-315; this is a feature for local injection but limits whole-body effects. Becker MD patients had heterogeneous baseline function. IBM is notoriously hard to treat — modest benefit is still notable.

Narrative: Heart disease is the world's top killer, and its chief accomplice is LDL cholesterol. For decades, statins have been the answer — but they require daily pills for life, and many patients stop taking them. Nature, however, has already solved the compliance problem. In 2006, Hobbs and Cohen discovered that roughly 3% of African Americans carry loss-of-function mutations in PCSK9, a liver enzyme that destroys LDL receptors. These carriers had LDL levels 28% lower and an 88% reduction in coronary heart disease — with no apparent downside. In 2021, Verve Therapeutics used lipid nanoparticle-delivered adenine base editing to knock out PCSK9 in cynomolgus monkeys with a single intravenous infusion: LDL dropped 59% and stayed down for over a year with no booster. In 2025, their phase Ib human trial (VERVE-102) showed durable 39–55% LDL reductions in heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia patients already on maximally tolerated therapy. One injection, permanent cholesterol lowering, no daily pills. The catch: liver base editing is irreversible — any off-target edits in hepatocytes are permanent, and the long-term cancer risk of widespread somatic editing remains theoretical but unquantified.
Short Description: A single base edit that permanently knocks out PCSK9 in the liver, lowering LDL cholesterol for life without daily pills. Monkey studies showed 59% LDL reduction lasting over a year, and early human trials confirm durable effects.
Mechanism: Adenine base editor (ABE) delivered via LNP to hepatocytes; introduces A→G transition creating splice-site disruption in PCSK9 exon 1; loss of functional PCSK9 protein → LDL receptors on hepatocyte surface persist instead of being degraded → increased LDL-C clearance from plasma.
Achievements (effect sizes): Cohen 2006 NEJM: PCSK9 LOF carriers — LDL −28%, CHD risk −88% (15-year follow-up, n=3363 Black participants). Musunuru 2021 Nature (monkey, n=4): single LNP-ABE dose → PCSK9 protein −90%, LDL-C −59% at 8 months, stable. VERVE-102 2025 (phase Ib, n=12 HeFH): LDL reductions 39–55% at highest dose, durable through 180 days; no serious liver events.
Translational Gaps: Phase III efficacy/safety (MACE endpoints); long-term off-target somatic editing surveillance; immunogenicity of LNP on repeat dosing (if ever needed); applicability beyond FH to general population; liver cancer risk from widespread base editing.
Key References (DOIs): Cohen NEJM 2006 https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa054013 | Musunuru Nature 2021 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-03534-y | Verve Therapeutics 2025 AHA Late-Breaking https://doi.org/10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.124.073624
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: PCSK9-null humans (compound LOF homozygotes) are healthy, supporting safety — but sample size is tiny (2 reported individuals). LNP tropism is liver-dominant but not liver-exclusive; low-level editing in other organs is possible. Base editing off-targets are harder to detect than CRISPR indels. Cohen 2006 CHD reduction (88%) may overestimate causal effect due to lifelong exposure vs mid-life intervention. Verve trials use next-gen LNP (GalNAc-targeted) to improve hepatocyte specificity.

Narrative: Belgian Blue cattle look like bodybuilders because they are — naturally. A deletion in their myostatin gene, discovered by McPherron and Lee at Johns Hopkins in 1997, removes the molecular brake on muscle growth. Without functional myostatin, skeletal muscle proliferates dramatically: Belgian Blues carry 20–25% more muscle fibers than normal cattle. The same biology works across species. In 2007, Mosher identified a myostatin mutation in racing Whippets — dogs heterozygous for the deletion were the fastest racers, while homozygotes were comically over-muscled 'bully Whippets'. In 2004, Schuelke reported the first known human myostatin mutation: a German boy born to a former professional sprinter carried a splice-site mutation in both MSTN alleles and showed visible muscle hypertrophy at birth, with quadriceps cross-section nearly twice normal at age four. He was otherwise healthy. The therapeutic angle is clear — blocking myostatin could treat muscular dystrophies, sarcopenia, and cachexia. Multiple antibody therapies have entered clinical trials, and CRISPR-mediated MSTN knockout has been demonstrated in pigs, dogs, and primates. The catch: myostatin also regulates tendons, cardiac muscle, and metabolism, and hyper-muscular animals often have calving difficulties and altered fat distribution.
Short Description: Myostatin is the body's brake on muscle growth. Natural knockouts in cattle, dogs, and one human child show dramatic muscle hypertrophy with few health consequences, making MSTN disruption a compelling target for treating muscle-wasting diseases.
Mechanism: Myostatin (GDF-8): secreted TGF-beta family ligand; binds ActRIIB → SMAD2/3 phosphorylation → repression of MyoD/myogenin transcription and satellite cell proliferation. Loss-of-function → unrestricted myoblast proliferation and myofiber hypertrophy; fiber count increases (hyperplasia) and fiber size increases (hypertrophy).
Achievements (effect sizes): McPherron 1997 Nature: MSTN-null mice → muscle mass +200–300% vs wild-type (individual muscles 2–3× heavier). Belgian Blue cattle: +20–25% muscle fiber number (hyperplasia). Schuelke 2004 NEJM: human MSTN-null boy — quadriceps CSA ~2× normal at age 4.5; grossly muscular at birth. Mosher 2007: heterozygous Whippets were fastest racers; homozygous 'bully' phenotype. Lee 2012: MSTN-null mice had reduced body fat (~50% less adipose tissue).
Translational Gaps: No human gene-therapy trial for MSTN-KO; antibody therapies (domagrozumab, stamulumab) had disappointing phase II results in DMD; CRISPR-KO in primates showed mosaicism; tendon/ligament adaptation to hyper-muscular loads; cardiac effects of systemic MSTN loss; long-term metabolic consequences.
Key References (DOIs): McPherron Nature 1997 https://doi.org/10.1038/387083a0 | Schuelke NEJM 2004 https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa040933 | Mosher PLoS Genet 2007 https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pgen.0030079
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: Myostatin antibodies (stamulumab, domagrozumab) FAILED to show clinical benefit in DMD phase II trials — systemic myostatin inhibition may not be enough when muscle is already dystrophic. Hyper-muscular cattle have dystocia (difficult births) and Belgian Blues require near-universal C-sections. MSTN-null mice have altered glucose metabolism and reduced adipose, which could be benefit or risk depending on context. The one known human case is a single individual — long-term health outcomes unknown. CRISPR-edited MSTN-KO pigs and dogs show variable mosaicism.

Narrative: Your heart is starving for blood — arteries clogged beyond what stents or bypasses can reach — and a surgeon injects naked DNA encoding VEGF directly into the dying muscle. That was the audacious premise when Losordo and Isner ran the first phase I trial in 1998: five men with intractable angina received intramyocardial injections of phVEGF165 plasmid. All five showed reduced angina, improved perfusion on SPECT imaging, and better collateral vessel scores on angiography. The same team demonstrated collateral artery growth in critical limb ischemia patients, with ankle-brachial index rising from 0.33 to 0.48 and limb salvage in three patients facing amputation. Then came the reality check. The Euroinject One phase II randomised double-blind trial in 2005 — 80 patients, plasmid VEGF versus placebo — found no significant improvement in myocardial perfusion, though wall motion did improve. The pattern has repeated across multiple trials: real biology, measurable protein expression, but clinical endpoints stubbornly close to placebo. VEGF gene therapy is safe and biologically active, but durable clinical benefit remains unproven after two decades of effort.
Short Description: VEGF gene therapy aims to grow new blood vessels in starved heart or limb tissue by injecting DNA encoding a vascular growth factor. Early trials showed promise, but larger placebo-controlled studies have not confirmed lasting clinical benefit.
Mechanism: VEGF-A165 isoform: secreted homodimeric glycoprotein; binds VEGFR-2/KDR on endothelial cells → PLCγ/ERK + PI3K/Akt → proliferation, migration, tube formation, nitric oxide release; also increases vascular permeability via VE-cadherin phosphorylation.
Achievements (effect sizes): Losordo 1998 Circulation (phase I, n=5): all 5 patients reduced NTG use 54→10/wk, improved SPECT perfusion. Baumgartner 1998 Circulation (phase I limb, n=9): ABI 0.33→0.48, new collaterals 7/10, limb salvage 3/3 amputation candidates. Euroinject One 2005 JACC (phase II RCT, n=80): NO significant perfusion improvement vs placebo; wall motion improved (P=0.04).
Translational Gaps: Transient plasmid expression (~2 weeks); no phase III with hard endpoints (MACE/mortality); AAV vs plasmid comparison needed; VEGF dose–response and isoform optimisation; edema management.
Key References (DOIs): Losordo Circulation 1998 https://doi.org/10.1161/01.cir.98.25.2800 | Baumgartner Circulation 1998 https://doi.org/10.1161/01.cir.97.12.1114 | Kastrup JACC 2005 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacc.2004.12.068
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: PLACEBO EFFECT dominates: Euroinject One showed equivalent CCS improvement in VEGF and placebo arms. Transient expression from naked plasmid may be insufficient for durable vessel maturation. Edema is consistent adverse event. VEGF can promote pathological angiogenesis (tumour vascularisation, retinopathy) — though clinical follow-up has NOT confirmed these risks. Multiple large programmes were abandoned after phase II failures.

Narrative: One woman in Colombia should have lost her mind by her forties. She carried PSEN1-E280A, a mutation that guarantees early-onset Alzheimer's in the world's largest autosomal dominant kindred — over 6,000 family members around Medellín. Instead, she remained cognitively intact until her seventies, three decades past the expected onset. The reason, reported by Arboleda-Velasquez in Nature Medicine in 2019, was two copies of an extraordinarily rare APOE variant called Christchurch (R136S). Her brain was loaded with amyloid but had remarkably little tau pathology and neurodegeneration. In 2018, Huang's group at Gladstone had already shown that converting APOE4 to APOE3 by CRISPR in human iPSC-derived neurons rescued tau phosphorylation, amyloid-beta overproduction, and GABAergic neuron loss. In 2023, Nelson and Huang engineered the R136S mutation into APOE4 mice and iPSC neurons and found that homozygous Christchurch edit rescued APOE4-driven tau pathology, neurodegeneration, and neuroinflammation. The idea of editing a patient's APOE4 alleles to a protective form is now the sharpest genetic target in Alzheimer's prevention. The catch: nobody has delivered a brain-wide APOE edit in a living animal, and APOE2 homozygosity itself raises cardiovascular lipid risk.
Short Description: APOE4 is the strongest genetic risk factor for Alzheimer's, and rare protective variants like APOE2 and the Christchurch mutation dramatically reduce disease. Gene editing to convert a patient's APOE4 to a protective form is compelling but has never been achieved in a living brain.
Mechanism: APOE: 299-aa secreted glycoprotein; E4 (Arg112/Arg158), E3 (Cys112/Arg158), E2 (Cys112/Cys158); Christchurch = R136S. E4 promotes Aβ aggregation, impairs Aβ clearance via LRP1/HSPG, increases tau phosphorylation. E2/Christchurch reduce HSPG binding, preserve microglial lipid homeostasis, diminish neuroinflammation.
Achievements (effect sizes): Wang 2018 Nat Med: iPSC APOE4/4 neurons showed increased p-tau, increased Aβ42/40, GABAergic neuron loss; CRISPR E4→E3 RESCUED all phenotypes. Nelson 2023 Nat Neurosci: APOE4-R136S homozygous in PS19 tauopathy mice rescued tau pathology, ~40% hippocampal volume loss prevented, neuroinflammation reduced. Arboleda-Velasquez 2019 Nat Med: PSEN1 carrier homozygous for APOE3-Christchurch — clinical onset delayed ~30 years.
Translational Gaps: No in-vivo brain-wide APOE editing in any animal; AAV/LNP BBB delivery; off-target base editing; APOE2/2 dysbetalipoproteinaemia risk (~5–10% of E2 homozygotes); mosaicism concerns.
Key References (DOIs): Wang Nat Med 2018 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-018-0004-z | Arboleda-Velasquez Nat Med 2019 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-019-0611-3 | Nelson Nat Neurosci 2023 https://doi.org/10.1038/s41593-023-01480-8
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: APOE2 HOMOZYGOSITY carries its own risk: ~5–10% develop type III hyperlipoproteinaemia. Christchurch mutation is extraordinarily rare — single-case evidence. The Colombian woman had APOE3 background, not APOE4; whether R136S rescues E4 in humans is inferred from mice only. APOE4 effects are species-specific: E4 increased Aβ in human but NOT mouse neurons. Heterozygous R136S only partially protective.

Narrative: What if aging isn't one disease but a cascade of interconnected failures — and you could treat several at once with a single injection? In 2019, Noah Davidsohn and George Church's group at the Wyss Institute tested exactly that. They packaged three longevity-associated genes — FGF21, αKlotho, and a soluble decoy of TGF-beta receptor 2 (sTGFβR2) — into separate AAV vectors and delivered them to mice modelling obesity, diabetes, heart failure, and kidney disease. A single combination of just FGF21 plus sTGFβR2 treated all four conditions simultaneously: heart function improved 58%, kidney fibrosis marker αSMA dropped 38%, renal atrophy fell 75%, and obesity with diabetes was completely reversed in high-fat-diet mice. FGF21 alone had already been shown to extend lifespan — Zhang et al. in 2012 demonstrated that FGF21-transgenic mice lived about 36% longer by suppressing the growth-hormone/IGF-1 axis, mimicking caloric restriction without eating less. The TGFβR2 decoy mops up excess TGF-beta signalling, the master driver of fibrosis in aged kidneys, livers, and hearts. The catch: Davidsohn's study used disease-specific mouse models, not natural aging, and the AAV doses were high. Whether one shot in middle age can genuinely extend healthy lifespan in normal mice remains untested.
Short Description: FGF21 and a soluble TGF-beta trap were combined in a single gene therapy that reversed obesity, diabetes, heart failure, and kidney fibrosis in mouse disease models. FGF21 alone extends mouse lifespan 36%. Whether the combination extends lifespan in naturally aging animals is still unknown.
Mechanism: FGF21: hepatokine (181 aa) signalling via FGFR1c/β-Klotho; suppresses GH/IGF-1 axis, enhances insulin sensitivity, promotes fatty acid oxidation. sTGFβR2: soluble decoy receptor sequestering TGF-β1/β3; blocks SMAD2/3 phosphorylation → reduces fibroblast activation, collagen deposition, and epithelial–mesenchymal transition.
Achievements (effect sizes): Davidsohn 2019 PNAS: AAV-FGF21 + AAV-sTGFβR2 in C57BL/6 mice: heart failure model → 58% improvement in fractional shortening; kidney fibrosis → 38% reduction in αSMA, 75% reduction in renal atrophy; HFD obesity/diabetes → complete reversal. Zhang 2012 eLife: FGF21-Tg mice median lifespan +36% (females ~46 vs ~34 months); mechanism via blunting GH/IGF-1.
Translational Gaps: Natural-aging lifespan study with combination not published; AAV dose optimisation; immunogenicity of chronic sTGFβR2; FGF21 resistance in metabolically stressed humans; human FGF21 analogues (pegbelfermin) showed mixed phase II results for NASH.
Key References (DOIs): Davidsohn PNAS 2019 https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1910073116 | Zhang eLife 2012 https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.00065
Notes (limitations, contradictions, cave: FGF21 lifespan extension correlated with SUPPRESSED GH/IGF-1 axis — FGF21-Tg mice had reduced body size (~20% smaller) and bone loss; effectively a caloric-restriction mimic with growth penalty. Davidsohn 2019 used disease-SPECIFIC models, NOT natural aging. sTGFβR2 blocks a pathway essential for immune regulation and wound healing — chronic suppression could impair anti-tumour immunity. FGF21 resistance reported in obese/inflamed humans. Church/Davidsohn co-founded Rejuvenate Bio (conflict of interest noted). No independent replication of the combination therapy.
